attributes.stem: A 65-year-old woman has recently been diagnosed with carcinoma of the right breast. The lesion was detected by self-examination. On clinical examination, there is a single 4 cm mass palpable in the upper outer quadrant of a G-cup size breast, adjacent to the nipple. Right axillary lymph nodes are enlarged and easily palpable. Mammogram and ultrasound show a 3 cm malignant mass in the right upper outer quadrant adjacent to the nipple. Multiple pathological axillary nodes are seen on right axillary ultrasound. Core biopsy of the breast lesion showed invasive ductal carcinoma of “no special type” (NST), grade 2, ER / PR positive, HER 2 negative and FNA of one of the axillary nodes confirms malignant cells.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following surgery-first strategies is most appropriate?
options: Breast conserving surgery and axillary node clearance
Total mastectomy and sentinel node biopsy
Total mastectomy and axillary node clearance
Breast conserving surgery and sentinel node biopsy
attributes.explanation:
This patient's breast cancer is likely locally advanced disease to the immediate lymph nodes and approximately stage IIIA. The first decision to be made is the type of surgical intervention: breast conserving surgery (BCS) vs mastectomy. There is extensive literature about the benefits of BCS in terms of psychological outcomes for the patient and quality of life. BCS is able to performed if: it is possible to excise the tumor to negative margins with an acceptable cosmetic outcome, patients must be able to receive radiotherapy, and must be suitable for follow-up to allow prompt detection of local recurrence. There are also relative indications such as tumour size < 5cm. As such, BCS is suitable in this patient. Important to note in all BCS cases adjuvant radiotherapy and/or hormonal treatment is required to minimise local and systemic disease recurrence.

The second management decision is deciding between sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) and axillary lymph node dissection (ALND). An absolute indication for axillary lymph node dissection is clinically detected nodes, as in this case. In an effort to reduce the adverse outcomes associated with ALND, such as lymphedema, SLNB is increasingly being performed in patients who are clinically node negative.

Lumpectomy and radiotherapy would not be appropriate in this case, as there is already locally advanced disease to axillary lymph nodes.
learningPoints: With current adjuvant therapies, there is a diminishing need for radical breast surgery.
Breast conserving surgery + radiotherapy has at least the same survival benefit as total mastectomy
references: Volders JH, Negenborn VL, Spronk PE, Krekel NMA, Schoonmade LJ, et al. Breast-conserving surgery following neoadjuvant therapy-a systematic review on surgical outcomes. Breast Cancer Res Treat. 2018;168(1):1-12. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29214416/

Newman LA. Decision Making in the Surgical Management of Invasive Breast Cancer-Part 1: Lumpectomy, Mastectomy, and Contralateral Prophylactic Mastectomy. Oncology (Williston Park). 2017;21(5):359-68. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28512732/
Moo TA, Sanford R, Dang C, Morrow M. Overview of breast cancer therapy. PET Clinics. 2018;13(3):339-354. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30100074/
